straight_beat.name: Lenard Chouteau
straight_beat.column:
The Cardinals won again. That’s the first thing to note. A 3-2 decision over the Red Sox, a team that’s been a thorn in St. Louis’ side for years. The scorecard’s neat, the box score’s tidy, but here’s the thing: this isn’t the first time they’ve done this. It’s the same old story. A win here, a loss there, and the standings? Still a mess. The Cardinal Way, for what it’s worth, is supposed to be about grit, about grinding, about not folding when the chips are down. But when the chips are down, they’re still folding.  

Look, I’m not here to harp on the standings. Not really. But the numbers don’t lie. Eight wins, five losses. A half-game lead in the division?

optimist.name: Gretchen Hartmann
optimist.column:
The Cardinals lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that’s the end of the story. No, no, no—this team’s got a way of making you believe in the impossible. You think an eight-game skid matters? Honey, the Cardinals invented the comeback. Molina could un-retire tomorrow and I’d believe it.  

Look, the Red Sox had their way with us yesterday, sure. They hit the ball hard, made the plays, and reminded us that even the best teams can falter. But here’s the thing: we’re still in this race. Eight wins, five losses. One game back in the NL Central. That’s not a chasm—it’s a driveway. And we’ve got a history of turning driveways into highways.  

I’ll tell you what I saw yesterday. Not the loss, but the moments that made me grin like a fool. That kid in the seventh inning, the one who’d barely made the majors last year, stepping into the box and delivering a pitch that made the crowd go wild. That’s the kind of magic that doesn’t fade. And when the team’s got a guy like that, you know the devil’s got a plan. Devil magic, baby.  

Sure, we’re not where we want to be. But let’s not forget where we’ve been. This isn’t the first time we’ve come back from the brink. And if you think the front office is done tinkering, you’re not paying attention. They’ve got a blueprint for this kind of season, and it’s written in red, white, and blue.  

The standings? They’re a mirror. They show

pessimist.name: Vern Kratzinger
pessimist.column:
The Cardinals lost again. Not to a team with a better record, not to a team that’s actually trying to win, but to a team that’s somehow still better than them. Boston’s 2-3 win over St. Louis feels like a reminder of how far the franchise has fallen. You’d think a 8-5 record would be cause for celebration, but in Cardinal Country, that’s just another day of pretending the rebuild is a vacation.  

The manager, Mike—yes, Mike again—has turned into a walking memo from the front office: “We’re not here to win, we’re here to… whatever.” The batting order yesterday was a masterclass in baffling decisions. Why is a guy who can’t hit a slider leading off? Why is the cleanup spot occupied by a player who’s hit more doubles than home runs since 2020? It’s not the Cardinal Way. The Cardinal Way is having a plan, not a PowerPoint presentation.  

And let’s talk about the bullpen. The man in the seventh inning, the one who’s been called upon to close games, is a relic from a different era. He’s throwing gasps instead of gas. The Cardinals’ relief corps is like a team of
